question: Statistical derivation question for an exam-readiness estimator. Setup: an exam draws a fixed quota of MCQ items per topic block (20 total, pass ≥18). For each STUDIED item we hold an FSRS retrievability estimate R_i built from that item's own review history (m_i spaced reviews; per-item posterior precision grows with m_i). Items within a topic share correlated knowledge (ICC ρ≈0.3), so per-item R estimates share MODEL error within a topic; some of each block's exam slots may correspond to material the student hasn't studied (extrapolation from block mean). The exam pass probability is computed by exact Poisson-binomial over per-item probabilities. PROBLEM with our current correction: we shrink each block's mean p̂ via Kish effective sample size n_eff = n_seen/(1+(n_seen−1)ρ) with a Jeffreys pseudo-posterior toward ½ — this treats each studied item as ONE Bernoulli draw, ignores per-item review counts m_i, and n_eff caps at 1/ρ forever, so a PERFECT student (all R=1, thousands of reviews) is stuck at dial ≈59% and the top readiness band is unreachable — structurally miscalibrated pessimism. REQUIRED: the statistically correct shrinkage/uncertainty propagation that (a) NEVER raises the dial above the independence baseline computed from raw R_i (honesty, keep), (b) RELEASES asymptotically — as per-item evidence m_i and coverage grow, the corrected dial must converge to the independence baseline (correction → 0), (c) still discounts sparse evidence hard (2 seen items must not certify an 11-slot block), (d) stays computable in closed form or small quadrature (no MCMC; feeds an exact PB recursion). Evaluate: two-level (reviews-within-item, items-within-topic) design effects; hierarchical Beta/logit-normal posterior-predictive for the block; shrinking only the UNSEEN-slot extrapolation while leaving well-estimated seen items unshrunk; credible-bound-with-evidence-decay schemes; and where within-topic shared MODEL error should instead be left to output-side calibration (Platt on real outcomes) rather than input-side shrinkage. Deliver: recommended formula set with the (a)+(b)+(c) properties proven (monotonicity + asymptotic-release proof sketches), the explicit formula for mixed seen/unseen blocks, and 4–6 worked numeric examples computable with scipy frozen as test oracles — including the release property (p̂=0.95, m→∞ ⇒ corrected p̃→0.95) and the sparse case (n_seen=2, quota 11).
summary: The current Kish n_eff = n_seen/(1+(n_seen−1)ρ) correction is a self-weighting survey-sampling approximation that treats each studied item as one equally-weighted Bernoulli draw with no per-unit precision term, so it provably saturates at n_eff = 1/ρ (~3.33 at ρ=0.3) and can never release — the perfect-student pessimism is structural, not a tuning issue. The statistically correct replacement is a two-level hierarchical model: per-item Beta/logit-normal posteriors built from each item's m_i reviews (whose credible-bound gap shrinks deterministically at m^(−1/2), giving property (b) release), combined with a block-level latent common factor that carries the ICC as a MIXTURE over a shared factor rather than a shrinkage of means — conditional on the factor, items are independent, so the exact Poisson-binomial recursion applies at each of ~20 Gauss–Hermite nodes and the pass probability is the node-weighted average (property (d), no MCMC). Lahiri–Mukherjee's theorems supply the (b)+(c) proof skeleton: the HB block estimate is exactly f·ȳ_seen + (1−f)·C, cleanly separating unshrunk seen items from the unseen-slot extrapolation C (the posterior-predictive mean), and for the Bernoulli/logit-normal case C → ȳ_seen as evidence grows while remaining wide at n_seen=2, so 2 items never certify an 11-slot block; property (a) honesty holds because mixing over the factor only widens the score distribution (PB variance is maximized under homogenization, and Hoeffding's inequality shows mean-collapsing raises every lower-tail probability), keeping the dial at or below the independence baseline for pass thresholds above the mean. Within-topic shared MODEL error (FSRS miscalibration common to a topic) should NOT be double-counted input-side: FSRS calibration against real outcomes is a measured quantity, and the correct home for residual shared error is output-side Platt scaling on real exam outcomes — noting Platt beats isotonic below ~200–1000 outcomes but actively hurts if stacked on an input correction that already fixes the bias.

findings.claim: Diagnosis confirmed: the current n_eff = n_seen/(1+(n_seen−1)ρ) is Kish's clustering design effect deff = 1+(n̄−1)ρ, derived specifically for SELF-WEIGHTING designs (equal-probability srs-within-srs) where each elementary unit is a single equally-weighted observation with no per-unit measurement-precision term; consequently n_eff = n/deff saturates exactly at 1/ρ (≈3.33 at ρ=0.3: 1.54 at n=2, 3.26 at n=100, asymptote 3.33), so the dial ceiling for a perfect student is a structural artifact of misapplying a survey-sampling special case, not a calibration constant to tune.
findings.evidence: Three independent sources give the identical formula: PMC10426793 Eq.2 (DeffKish = (1+cv²w)(1+(n̄−1)ρ), reducing to 1+(n̄−1)ρ under equal weights, with no replicate-within-unit precision term); Park & Lee 2004 (Statistics Canada, Survey Methodology) Eq.4.18 derives Deft² = 1+ρ(m−1) explicitly 'under a self-weighting design' relative to srswr and warns design effects do not generalize outside it; CRAN PracTools vignette states deff_clus = 1+ρ(n*−1) verbatim. The 1/ρ saturation is exact algebra (n_eff = 1/(1/n + ρ(1−1/n)) → 1/ρ), verified numerically in the verification pass. Gabler et al. additionally showed the Kish form is a conservative overestimate even within its own assumptions.

findings.claim: The correct TWO-STAGE design effect (the survey-sampling analog of reviews-within-item, items-within-topic) is Deft² ≅ 1+(m0−1)τ + W*_y, where τ replaces the raw ICC with a ratio mixing between-cluster variance and per-cluster within-cluster variances weighted by second-stage sample sizes — i.e., even within the design-effect framework, the proper two-level correction explicitly incorporates per-cluster evidence (the analog of m_i), which the flat Kish 1+(n−1)ρ discards. This is the design-effect-side confirmation that ignoring m_i is the root defect.
findings.evidence: Park & Lee 2004, Example 4.5, Eqs. 4.19–4.20, verified verbatim from the extracted PDF: τ = [(N−1)S²_yB + Σ(m0−1)⁻¹S²_yi]/[(N−1)S²_yB + Σ(Mᵢ−1)S²_yi], with the (m0−1)⁻¹ weight making the design effect depend on second-stage evidence. The paper explicitly contrasts this with Kish's flat formula and notes prior users 'obviously overlooked' the difference. Corroborated by Wang & Valliant (PMC10426793) citing it as the established two-stage extension. Caveat: derived for a specific pps-with-replacement design, so it motivates but does not itself supply the estimator's formula — the hierarchical Bayes route (next finding) does.

findings.claim: The recommended block formula and its (b)+(c) proof skeleton come from Lahiri & Mukherjee (Annals of Statistics 2007): the Bayes estimator of a block mean is EXACTLY f·ȳ_seen + (1−f)·E[ψ'(θ)|y_seen] at finite n — seen slots enter as the unshrunk sample mean, unseen slots as the posterior-predictive mean C — and (i) for the Bernoulli/logit-normal hierarchical model (the case matching per-item pass probabilities) C → ȳ_seen as within-block evidence grows, so shrinkage on the unseen-slot extrapolation vanishes asymptotically (property b); (ii) in the fully hierarchical multi-stratum case with unknown hyperparameters the HB stratum estimate provably converges to that stratum's own sample mean (per-block release); (iii) crucially, this release is NOT automatic for arbitrary Bayes constructions — subjective Bayes estimators are in general not design-consistent, so release must be engineered and verified (which is exactly what the Jeffreys-toward-½ pseudo-posterior fails to do). Mixed seen/unseen formula: p̃_block-slot(unseen) = posterior-predictive mean of the Beta/logit-normal block model given the m_i-weighted per-item posteriors; seen items keep their own per-item posteriors unshrunk.
findings.evidence: All four facets verified verbatim against the full paper (peer-reviewed, Ann. Statist. 35(2):724–737): Theorem 2.1 (Ŷ^B → f·ȳ_s + (1−f)C, with the finite-n decomposition exact via Eq. 2.3); Example 2 + Remark 2.1 (Bernoulli with logit link and normal prior: C = ψ'(T) = ȳ_s); Theorem 3.1 + Eq. 3.5 + Appendix proof (multi-stratum HB: E[ψ'(θ_m)|y_s] → ȳ_m as n_m grows, normal case with all hyperparameters unknown; Remark 3.1 gives C_m = ȳ_m for Bernoulli in the known-φ case); and the headline negative result ('the subjective Bayes estimator is, in general, not design-consistent'). Qualification: these are calculus limits with observations treated as fixed, and the multi-stratum unknown-hyperparameter proof is for the normal model — the Bernoulli analog is proven in the known-dispersion case.

findings.claim: The block-level model to use for the posterior-predictive is a hierarchical Beta-Binomial / logit-normal formulation: within-topic ICC among binary items manifests as binomial overdispersion, the Beta-Binomial is the standard remedy, and a concrete published small-area form exists — y_i|p_i ~ Binomial(n_i, p_i), p_i|v_i ~ Beta(α, γ), logit(p_i) = z_i'β + v_i, v_i ~ N(0, σ_v²) — matching the structure needed to estimate a topic-block proportion from few observed items. This is the vehicle for property (c): with n_seen=2 the Beta posterior-predictive for the 9 unseen slots of an 11-quota block stays wide (a Jeffreys-updated Beta(0.5+s, 0.5+f) on 2 items has posterior mass spanning most of [0,1]), so sparse blocks are hard-discounted, while the same machinery washes out as n_seen and m_i grow.
findings.evidence: The ICC→overdispersion→Beta-Binomial chain was verified as textbook-standard with corroboration from PMC5736152 and standard references; the concrete model equations were verified against the journal of record (Media Statistika 16(1):88–99, 2023, Sunandi et al., downloaded full PDF), including the v_i ~ N(0, σ_v²) area effect, applied to exactly the few-observations block-proportion problem. Caveat: the published model covers one hierarchy level (areas); the reviews-within-item level must be added by feeding m_i-precise per-item posteriors in as the within-block observations (the bridging is ours, supported by the two-stage design-effect and Lahiri–Mukherjee findings).

findings.claim: The correct ARCHITECTURE for the ICC is a one-factor mixture, not mean shrinkage: model within-topic correlation via a latent common factor Y such that items are conditionally independent given Y; then (i) conditional on each factor value the count distribution is exactly the heterogeneous Poisson-binomial over the per-item R_i(y), so the existing exact PB recursion is reused unchanged at each node; (ii) the outer mixture is computable with ~20-node Gauss–Hermite quadrature for a Gaussian factor (tanh-sinh otherwise) — closed-form-adjacent, no MCMC, satisfying property (d); (iii) by the conditional LLN, as the COUNT of correlated items grows the block fraction converges to the random variable p(Y), not the constant p — quantifying exactly why n_eff saturates at 1/ρ and proving that release (property b) can only come from per-item evidence m_i shrinking each item's posterior and the factor-variance posterior, never from n_seen alone.
findings.evidence: All three components verified verbatim from the NAG technical report (Navas-Palencia, TR1/16, Vasicek one-factor credit model — canonical mathematics): conditional independence given the systematic factor with binomial/PB conditional counts; 'Gauss–Hermite quadrature with 20 nodes' used for the mixture integrals with tanh-sinh named for non-Gaussian cases (independently corroborated by GLMM/IRT adaptive-GH practice and arXiv math/0506378); Assumption 4.1's conditional-LLN limit to p(Y). The saturation link was verified numerically in the verification pass (Monte Carlo beta-mixed block variance plateaus at Var(p(Y)) ≈ 0.05 instead of vanishing; Kish n_eff → 3.33 at ρ=0.3). Two scoped caveats from the 2-1 vote on the representation claim: 'exactly' holds for the exchangeable positive-ρ case relevant here (de Finetti), but a one-factor model cannot represent negative correlation and does not pin down higher-order dependence beyond mean+ICC.
findings.vote: 2-1, 3-0, 3-0 (merged claims 9, 10, 11; the split vote is on the word 'exactly', with high-confidence verifier evidence for the positive-ρ exchangeable case)

findings.claim: Why the current correction is provably pessimistic and where the ICC must live relative to the PB step: (i) the Poisson-binomial is defined strictly for INDEPENDENT Bernoullis — the exact PB recursion has no correlation provision, so ICC handling must be input-side (factor mixture) or output-side (calibration), never inside the recursion; (ii) for fixed mean, Var(X) = np̄(1−p̄) − Σ(p_i−p̄)², so homogenizing per-item probabilities toward a block mean strictly increases score variance (maximized at the binomial); (iii) Hoeffding's inequality (P(X≤k) ≤ P(X̄≤k) for k ≤ np̄−1) proves mean-collapsing raises every relevant lower-tail probability — numerically verified: for n=20, p̄=0.943, heterogeneous P(X≤17)=0.1003 vs mean-collapsed 0.1038 — so mean-shrinkage corrections structurally lower P(pass ≥18) for high-mean students relative to the independence baseline built from raw R_i. This simultaneously establishes property (a) for the factor-mixture design: mixing/homogenizing only widens the score distribution, so the corrected dial sits at or below the independence baseline for pass thresholds above the mean score.
findings.evidence: All three verified against Tang & Tang (expository survey, later Statistical Science): the independence-only PB definition (Eq. 1.1 and the product-form PGF underlying all exact algorithms); the variance identity Eq. 2.1 with 'maximized as p1=···=pn', confirmed algebraically and numerically (variance rises monotonically 0.650→1.233 as shrinkage λ goes 1→0 in the verifier's sweep); Theorem 2.1 (Hoeffding 1956) verbatim with the exact k-range, plus the direct numeric check of the pass-probability direction at k=17, n=20. Scope caveat carried from verification: the variance mechanism as proven applies to mean-PRESERVING shrinkage toward the block mean; the current Jeffreys-toward-½ correction also shifts the mean (a second, additive source of pessimism for above-½ students).

findings.claim: Credible-bound-with-evidence-decay schemes have the required quantitative release rate: the expected gap between a one-sided Jeffreys/score credible limit and the true mean shrinks deterministically as E(U−μ) = κ(μ+bμ²)^{1/2} m^{−1/2} + O(m^{−1}) (Cai 2005, Theorem 5), so a lower-credible-bound dial converges to the point-estimate (independence-baseline) dial as per-item review counts m_i grow — usable as the per-item evidence-decay term feeding the factor mixture. However, the stronger claims that the Jeffreys bound is first-order probability-matched with 'insignificant' O(1/n) residual bias were REFUTED in verification (votes 1-2 and 0-3), so a credible-bound scheme should be treated as a conservative engineering device with the proven m^{−1/2} decay rate, not as an exactly calibrated bound.
findings.evidence: Theorem 5 / Eq. 22 verified verbatim against the published JSPI 131 (2005) 63–88 paper, holding for all four one-sided limits including Jeffreys, for iid Bin(1,p) draws. Confidence is medium because (a) the vote was 2-1, (b) two adjacent claims from the same paper about Jeffreys probability-matching failed verification, and (c) the theorem assumes iid fixed-mean draws whereas FSRS reviews have time-varying retrievability and it covers per-item sampling error only, not shared within-topic model error — all flagged by the verifier as non-refuting but real scope limits.

findings.claim: Within-topic shared MODEL error (FSRS miscalibration common to a topic, as opposed to per-item sampling noise) belongs on the OUTPUT side: (i) FSRS retrievability is explicitly benchmarked as a probability against real review outcomes (Log Loss + binned RMSE for calibration, AUC for discrimination), so recalibrating it against outcomes is a well-defined operation; (ii) with scarce real exam outcomes (< roughly 200–1000 held-out cases) Platt scaling is the right calibrator — it outperforms isotonic regression in exactly that regime; (iii) but Platt must NOT be stacked on top of an input-side correction that already fixes the bias — calibrating an already-calibrated system yields no benefit and actively hurts with small calibration sets. Division of labor: factor mixture handles the ICC/variance structure input-side; a single Platt layer on real exam/mock outcomes absorbs residual shared model error; never both for the same error component.
findings.evidence: All three verified verbatim: the srs-benchmark README's calibration/discrimination metric definitions (primary, actively maintained); Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana ICML 2005 for both the small-set Platt-beats-isotonic result ('less than about 200-1000 cases... all nine learning methods') and the stacking warning ('calibration is not beneficial, and actually hurts performance when the calibration sets are small' for already-calibrated models — quote verified including the authentic typo). Caveats from verification: the srs-benchmark measures per-review recall calibration, not readiness-dial calibration, so Platt on the dial needs real exam/mock outcomes rather than review logs; the 200–1000 threshold is an empirical rule of thumb; Platt should be fit on logits of probability-valued inputs.
